Case title: Project APE autonomy gradient: initialization, course correction, and revision mode
Short description: Exploit APE's own autonomy framing to compare minimal prompts, guided prompts, optional course correction, and human-triggered revision as distinct human intervention points.
Empirical unit of analysis: APE sessions, initialization instructions, revision instructions, and paper versions
Observable human actors: Human initializer, human reviser, project operator
Candidate roles tested: Relevance adjudicator; accountability and labor allocator; protocol and provenance architect
Key typology question: Are human roles in autonomous research mostly moved to initialization and revision rather than removed?
Observable artifacts / traces: Initialization prompts, revision requests, version diffs, autonomy documentation
Key outcomes observed: Error reduction, revision gains, dependence on human steering, role concentration at the edges
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Minimal-prompt and heavily guided outputs converge in quality and content
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Internal project design choices may not generalize to other autonomous-research systems
Why this case has high leverage: APE explicitly documents where humans still intervene, which most autonomous-research systems do not.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
